methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $5M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Artimus Robotics is a Seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Eric Acome that has raised $5M+. The valuation exercise draws from a comp universe of 9 private robotics firms and 1 public comparable (277810.KQ), though narrow-filter peer matching proved limited for this early-stage company.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable approach. Using the $5M total raised as a proxy for entry valuation and applying a standard 2.5x Seed-to-next-stage multiplier yields an implied valuation of $12M. A peer-median approach was not viable; while 11 robotics and manufacturing companies exist in the broader sector, none met the filters for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier comparables—a minimum of three datapoints is required for meaningful triangulation. The broader all-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given heterogeneity in robotics subsegments and business models.

The triangulated range is $11M to $14M, centered at $12M. This narrow band reflects reliance on a single quantitative method and limited peer specificity. Key uncertainties include team execution risk at early stage, the specific robotics subsegment focus (automation, hardware, software integration), and capital efficiency relative to the stated $5M raise. Investors should weight subsequent funding pace and technical milestones heavily, as stage-progression multiples assume normal venture progression rather than company-specific traction.
